core_concept.principle: Hazard symbols are universal visual warnings that communicate the dangers of chemicals quickly and clearly, regardless of language.
core_concept.explanation: Chemical hazard symbols protect you by providing instant information about risks like flammability, toxicity, or corrosion. Learning to recognize and respond to these symbols is essential for safe laboratory practice.

image.alt: Collection of GHS hazard pictograms with labels
image.caption: GHS pictograms provide standardized hazard communication worldwide.

sections.introduction: Chemicals can pose serious risks if mishandled. Hazard symbols act as a universal 'language of safety' that warns you about dangers before you even open a container.

introduction_image.alt: Student reading chemical label with hazard symbols
introduction_image.caption: Always check labels for hazard symbols before using any chemical.

key_concept.definition: Hazard symbols (pictograms) are standardized images on chemical labels that indicate specific types of danger, such as fire, health hazards, or environmental damage.
key_concept.context: The Globally Harmonized System (GHS) standardizes these symbols worldwide, so a flame means 'flammable' whether you're in Canada, Japan, or Brazil.

image.alt: GHS diamond with nine hazard pictograms
image.caption: The GHS system ensures consistent hazard communication across borders.

sections.introduction: The Globally Harmonized System (GHS) defines nine core pictograms. Each has a specific meaning, border color, and associated hazards.

classifications.value: Flammables, self-reactives, pyrophorics, self-heating substances, organic peroxides
characteristics: Red diamond border with black flame on white background
Indicates substances that ignite easily
Keep away from heat, sparks, open flames
classifications.behavior: These chemicals can catch fire with minimal ignition source; store in flammable cabinets.

examples.explanation: Common laboratory alcohol; vapors can ignite at room temperature.

image.caption: Flame = Flammable hazard.

examples.explanation: Highly volatile solvent; use in well-ventilated area away from ignition sources.

image.caption: Always check labels for the flame symbol.

classifications.value: Oxidizing substances that intensify fire or cause explosion
characteristics: Red diamond with flame above a circle
Indicates substances that provide oxygen to fuel fires
Can cause combustible materials to ignite spontaneously
classifications.behavior: Store separately from flammables; never mix with organic materials.

examples.explanation: Releases oxygen; can cause organic materials to burst into flame.

image.caption: Flame over circle = Oxidizing hazard.

classifications.value: Gases under pressure (compressed, liquefied, dissolved, or refrigerated)
characteristics: Red diamond with black gas cylinder
Indicates risk of explosion if heated or damaged
May cause cryogenic burns if refrigerated
classifications.behavior: Secure cylinders upright; use regulators; keep caps on when not in use.

examples.explanation: High-pressure gas; cylinder can become a projectile if valve breaks.

image.caption: Gas cylinder = Pressure hazard.

classifications.value: Substances that destroy skin, eyes, or metals upon contact
characteristics: Red diamond with test tubes pouring liquid onto hand and metal
Indicates severe skin burns, eye damage, or metal corrosion
Includes strong acids and bases
classifications.behavior: Wear gloves, goggles, and lab coat; rinse immediately if contact occurs.

examples.explanation: Concentrated acid causes severe burns; always add acid to water.

image.caption: Corrosion = Skin/eye damage or metal corrosion.

examples.explanation: Strong base; dissolves organic tissue; handle with extreme care.

image.caption: Bases can be as corrosive as acids.

classifications.value: Acute toxicity: fatal or toxic if swallowed, inhaled, or absorbed through skin
characteristics: Red diamond with black skull and crossbones
Indicates substances that can cause death or serious harm in small amounts
Requires strict handling protocols
classifications.behavior: Use in fume hood; wear full PPE; never work alone; know emergency procedures.

examples.explanation: Extremely toxic; interferes with cellular respiration; lethal in milligrams.

image.caption: Skull and crossbones = Acute toxicity hazard.

classifications.value: Irritant, skin sensitizer, acute toxicity (harmful), narcotic effects
characteristics: Red diamond with black exclamation mark
Indicates less severe but still significant health hazards
Includes substances that cause irritation or allergic reactions
classifications.behavior: Avoid skin contact and inhalation; use in ventilated area; wash after handling.

examples.explanation: Irritates eyes, skin, and respiratory tract; use in fume hood.

image.caption: Exclamation mark = Irritant or harmful hazard.

classifications.value: Carcinogen, mutagen, reproductive toxin, respiratory sensitizer, target organ toxicity
characteristics: Red diamond with black silhouette of a person with starburst on chest
Indicates long-term or chronic health effects
Effects may not be immediately apparent
classifications.behavior: Minimize exposure; use engineering controls; follow disposal protocols strictly.

examples.explanation: Known carcinogen; linked to leukemia; avoid inhalation and skin contact.

image.caption: Health hazard = Chronic or serious health effects.

classifications.value: Aquatic toxicity: harmful to water organisms with long-lasting effects
characteristics: Red diamond with dead tree and fish
Indicates substances toxic to aquatic life
Requires special disposal to prevent water contamination
classifications.behavior: Never pour down sink; collect waste for proper hazardous disposal.

examples.explanation: Copper, lead, or mercury ions harm aquatic ecosystems; collect all waste.

image.caption: Environment = Hazardous to aquatic life.

characteristics: Red diamond with black exploding bomb
Indicates substances that can detonate or explode under certain conditions
Requires specialized storage and handling
classifications.behavior: Store in explosive cabinets; avoid shock, friction, heat; follow teacher instructions exactly.

examples.explanation: When dry, becomes shock-sensitive explosive; always keep wet as directed.

image.caption: Exploding bomb = Explosion hazard.

sections.section_title: 1.5.3 Reading Chemical Labels: The GHS Label Elements
sections.introduction: A complete GHS label includes more than just the pictogram. Understanding all label elements ensures you handle chemicals safely.

principles.statement: 'Danger' indicates more severe hazards; 'Warning' indicates less severe hazards.
principles.application: Always note the signal word—it tells you the urgency of the risk.
principles.analogy: Like traffic signs: 'Danger' is a red stop sign; 'Warning' is a yellow caution sign.

image.alt: GHS label showing Danger vs Warning
image.caption: Signal words indicate hazard severity level.

principles.statement: Standardized phrases describing the nature and degree of hazard (e.g., 'Causes severe skin burns').
principles.application: Read hazard statements to understand exactly what risk the chemical poses.
principles.analogy: Like a weather forecast: it tells you specifically what to expect (rain, snow, wind).

image.caption: Hazard statements provide specific risk descriptions.

principles.statement: Recommended measures to minimize or prevent adverse effects (Prevention, Response, Storage, Disposal).
principles.application: Follow precautionary statements exactly—they are your safety instructions.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe's safety notes: 'Use oven mitts' tells you how to avoid burns.

image.alt: Label with P-codes and safety instructions
image.caption: Precautionary statements tell you how to stay safe.

sections.section_title: 1.5.4 Applying Knowledge: Identifying Hazards in the Lab
sections.introduction: Let's practice recognizing hazards and selecting appropriate precautions using real-world chemical examples.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing a Chemical Label
worked_examples.problem: You are given a bottle labeled with the Corrosion pictogram, Signal Word: Danger, and Hazard Statement: 'Causes severe skin burns and eye damage.' What precautions must you take before using this chemical?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the hazard type from the pictogram: Corrosion = skin/eye damage or metal corrosion.
Note the signal word: 'Danger' indicates a severe hazard requiring strict precautions.
Read precautionary statements on label (e.g., 'Wear protective gloves/eye protection').
step_by_step.calculation: PPE Required: Goggles, lab coat, chemical-resistant gloves

step_by_step.explanation: Plan safe handling: work in well-ventilated area, have eyewash accessible, know emergency rinse procedure.
step_by_step.calculation: Procedure: Use small amounts; rinse immediately if contact occurs

worked_examples.final_answer: Wear goggles, lab coat, and gloves; work in ventilated area; keep eyewash accessible; rinse immediately with water for 15+ minutes if contact occurs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hazard symbols and label elements guide risk assessment and precaution selection.

image.alt: Annotated chemical label with hazard elements highlighted
image.caption: Systematic label reading ensures safe chemical use.

worked_examples.title: Choosing Storage for Multiple Chemicals
worked_examples.problem: You have three chemicals: ethanol (Flame), hydrogen peroxide (Flame Over Circle), and hydrochloric acid (Corrosion). How should they be stored safely?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify incompatibilities: oxidizers (H₂O₂) can cause flammables (ethanol) to ignite violently.
Separate oxidizers from flammables: store hydrogen peroxide in oxidizer cabinet, ethanol in flammable cabinet.
step_by_step.calculation: Storage: H₂O₂ → Oxidizer cabinet; Ethanol → Flammable cabinet

step_by_step.explanation: Store acids separately from bases and organics: hydrochloric acid goes in corrosive/acid cabinet.
step_by_step.calculation: Storage: HCl → Acid cabinet (vented, secondary containment)

step_by_step.explanation: Ensure all containers are tightly closed, labeled, and stored at appropriate heights (heavy items low).
step_by_step.calculation: General: Secure caps; label clearly; store heavy items on lower shelves

worked_examples.final_answer: Store each chemical in its compatible cabinet: flammables separate from oxidizers; acids separate from bases and organics.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Chemical compatibility and hazard class determine safe storage segregation.

image.alt: Lab storage cabinets labeled by hazard class
image.caption: Proper segregation prevents dangerous chemical interactions.

sections.introduction: Even with precautions, exposure can occur. Knowing immediate response steps minimizes injury severity.

categories.description: Immediate action is critical to limit tissue damage from acids or bases.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Water dilutes and removes the chemical; prolonged rinsing ensures complete removal from skin pores.

examples.explanation: Remove contaminated clothing; rinse under safety shower or sink for 15+ minutes; seek medical help.

image.alt: Student rinsing arm at safety shower
image.caption: Immediate, prolonged rinsing is essential for corrosive exposure.

categories.description: Move to fresh air immediately to reduce exposure concentration.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Fresh air dilutes inhaled toxins; rest prevents increased respiratory rate that could worsen exposure.

examples.explanation: Leave area immediately; breathe fresh air; notify teacher; seek medical evaluation if symptoms persist.

image.caption: Fresh air is the first aid for inhalation exposure.

categories.description: Eyes are extremely vulnerable; immediate, thorough irrigation is non-negotiable.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Continuous water flow flushes chemicals from eye surface and under eyelids; 15 minutes ensures deep cleaning.

examples.explanation: Hold eyelids open; rinse at eyewash station for 15+ minutes; do not rub; get medical attention immediately.

image.caption: Eyewash stations must be used immediately and for full duration.

sections.introduction: Proper disposal protects people, plumbing, and the environment. Never pour hazardous chemicals down the sink unless explicitly instructed.

process_flow.description: Check the label and SDS to determine hazard class and disposal requirements.
process_flow.mechanism: Different hazards require different waste streams: organic, aqueous, heavy metal, halogenated, etc.

image.alt: Student checking chemical label before disposal
image.caption: Always verify waste type before disposal.

process_flow.description: Pour waste only into containers labeled for that specific hazard class.
process_flow.mechanism: Mixing incompatible wastes (e.g., acid + cyanide) can generate toxic gas or explosion.
process_flow.result: Waste is segregated safely for professional treatment.

image.caption: Use only the container matching your waste's hazard class.

process_flow.description: Ensure waste container label includes contents, hazard, date, and your name.
process_flow.mechanism: Clear labeling allows safe handling by waste management personnel.
process_flow.result: Waste stream is traceable and compliant with regulations.

image.caption: Complete labeling is a legal and safety requirement.

process_flow.description: Inform your teacher when waste container is full or if uncertain about disposal.
process_flow.mechanism: Teachers coordinate with institutional hazardous waste programs for proper pickup.
process_flow.result: Waste is removed by licensed hazardous waste contractors.

image.caption: When in doubt, ask your instructor.

summary: Hazard symbols are universal warnings that communicate chemical dangers instantly. The GHS system standardizes nine pictograms for hazards like flammability, corrosion, toxicity, and environmental harm. Complete labels include signal words, hazard statements, and precautionary statements to guide safe handling. Always read labels before use, wear appropriate PPE, store chemicals by compatibility, and respond immediately to exposure with rinsing or fresh air. Proper hazardous waste disposal protects people and the planet. Hazard literacy is foundational to laboratory safety.
